data.title: PFFA: Not All Preferred ETFs Are Built Alike
data.description:
Preferred shares occupy a unique space in the capital structure; technically equity, but with bond-like features such as fixed dividends and seniority over common stock in the event of liquidation.

They typically don’t have voting rights, and their prices tend to be more stable than common shares but more volatile than traditional bonds. This hybrid helps nature makes them attractive for income-seeking investors, especially those looking to diversify beyond just stocks and bonds.

One underappreciated benefit of preferreds is how well they can fit inside an exchange-traded fund (ETF). With an ETF, investors gain access to a basket of preferred securities that trades like a stock and generally provides regular income, often monthly. This makes the asset class more liquid and accessible than buying individual preferred shares.

But not all preferred ETFs are built the same. While passive index strategies are efficient and low-cost when it comes to equities, we believe preferreds are one area where active management has a real edge.

This is a less liquid part of the market, often with unique call provisions, credit considerations, and structural quirks that simple market cap-weighting can’t account for.

The Virtus InfraCap U.S. Preferred Stock ETF (NYSEARCA:PFFA) was designed with these nuances in mind. It prioritizes yield and total return, using an actively managed strategy tailored to the complexities of the preferred space. Here’s a look at what’s under the hood.

What Makes PFFA Tick

Rather than buying the entire preferreds market, PFFA uses a mix of quantitative and qualitative analysis to pick what it believes are the most attractive securities available.

This means avoiding low-conviction, low-return holdings and focusing instead on positions with higher yield and better fundamentals.

One example: PFFA screens out negative yield-to-call securities. These are preferred shares trading above their call price where, if the issuer redeems them early, investors could lose money. By filtering those out, PFFA aims to sidestep unnecessary capital erosion.

PFFA also actively manages exposure between fixed- and floating-rate preferreds, scrutinizes credit ratings closely, and diversifies across sectors, which is important in a market often dominated by financial institutions.

A key differentiator is PFFA’s ability to use modest leverage, typically between 20% and 25%. This adds to the fund’s income potential. The strategy has supported steady and historically rising monthly distributions.

Finally, there also can be a significant tax advantage. Most of PFFA’s distributions qualify as Qualified Dividend Income (QDI), which means they’re taxed at a lower rate than interest income from corporate bonds. For top earners, that could mean a 20% tax rate versus 37%, plus the 3.8% Medicare surtax applied to both.

PFFA's Track Record Of Outperformance

The performance data speaks for itself. Over numerous time horizons, PFFA has consistently outpaced the S&P U.S. Preferred Stock Index and its Morningstar peer category average.

The growth of $10,000 invested since inception highlights the compounding effect of both capital appreciation and reinvested distributions. By March 2025, PFFA turned that into $16,027 versus $12,807 for the index, roughly a $3,200 gap in absolute dollars.

PFFA combines thoughtful preferred security selection, sector diversification, active interest rate risk management, and a structural edge via leverage, all in a tax-aware wrapper.

For income-focused investors in the preferred space, we believe PFFA shows why structure, selection, and strategy matter more than simply buying passive exposure.

Please consider the investment objectives, risks, charges and expenses of the Fund carefully before investing. The prospectus contains this and other information about the Fund. Contact us at 1-888-383-0553 or visit www.virtus.com for a copy of the Fund's prospectus. Read the prospectus carefully before you invest or send money.

Morningstar Disclosures: The Morningstar Rating™ for funds, or "star rating", is calculated for managed products (including mutual funds, variable annuity and variable life subaccounts, exchange-traded funds, closed-end funds, and separate accounts) with at least a three-year history. Exchange-traded funds and open-ended mutual funds are considered a single population for comparative purposes. It is calculated based on a Morningstar Risk-Adjusted Return measure that accounts for variation in a managed product's monthly excess performance, placing more emphasis on downward variations and rewarding consistent performance. The top 10% of products in each product category receive 5 stars, the next 22.5% receive 4 stars, the next 35% receive 3 stars, the next 22.5% receive 2 stars, and the bottom 10% receive 1 star. The Overall Morningstar Rating for a managed product is derived from a weighted average of the performance figures associated with its three-, five-, and 10-year (if applicable) Morningstar Rating metrics. The weights are: 100% three-year rating for 36-59 months of total returns, 60% five-year rating/40% three-year rating for 60-119 months of total returns, and 50% 10-year rating/30% five-year rating/20% three-year rating for 120 or more months of total returns. While the 10-year overall star rating formula seems to give the most weight to the 10-year period, the most recent three-year period actually has the greatest impact because it is included in all three rating periods. Ratings do not take into account the effects of sales charges and loads. © 2025 Morningstar, Inc. All rights reserved. A fee was paid to Morningstar to license the use of the Stars, Rankings, and Ratings.

Exchange-Traded Funds (ETF): The value of an ETF may be more volatile than the underlying portfolio of securities it is designed to track. The costs to the portfolio of owning shares of an ETF may exceed the cost of investing directly in the underlying securities. Preferred Stocks: Preferred stocks may decline in price, fail to pay dividends, or be illiquid. Leverage: When the Fund leverages its portfolio, the Fund may be less liquid and/or may liquidate positions at an unfavorable time, and the value of the Fund’s shares will be more volatile and sensitive to market movements. Non-Diversified: The portfolio is not diversified and may be more susceptible to factors negatively impacting its holdings to the extent the portfolio invests more of its assets in the securities of fewer issuers than would a diversified portfolio. Market Price/NAV: At the time of purchase and/or sale, an investor’s shares may have a market price that is above or below the fund’s NAV, which may increase the investor’s risk of loss. Market Volatility: The value of the securities in the portfolio may go up or down in response to the prospects of individual companies and/or general economic conditions. Local, regional, or global events such as war or military conflict, terrorism, pandemic, or recession could impact the portfolio, including hampering the ability of the portfolio’s manager(s) to invest its assets as intended. Prospectus: For additional information on risks, please see the fund’s prospectus. data.title: Unum: Another Key Milestone Supports Ongoing Multiple Expansion
data.description:
Shares of Unum Group (NYSE:UNM) have been an excellent performer over the past year, adding about 60% as the company's growth businesses are performing well while fears around its legacy, toxic insurance policies have lessened. On Tuesday, July 1st, Unum closed a significant transaction that further accelerates its transformation. I last covered Unum in March, reiterating shares as a "buy." Since then, they have added 3%, but the market is up 10%. With it closing its reinsurance deal, now is a good time to revisit UNM.

Fortitude Re Deal Reduces Closed Block Risk

On Tuesday, Unum closed its agreement with Fortitude Re. Under this reinsurance deal, Unum is ceding $3.4 billion of long-term care reserves to the reinsurer, which represents about 19% of the company's total in-force long-term care (LTC) block as well as 20% of its individual disability block. This deal will unlock about $100 million of capital with a $200 million capital hit on LTC offset by a $300 million disability benefit. Unum can take this capital and either redeploy it into its core business, where it is generating 20+% returns on equity, or return that capital to shareholders.

Frankly, I believe more of the value for UNM shareholders in this transaction is shedding the 19% of risk, more so than the $100 million capital release. I would still view this deal positively even if it were capital neutral. LTC insurance has been a disaster for essentially every player involved in the market, as policies were severely underpriced in the 1990s-2000s. This has led companies like UNM to segregate LTC into a "closed block" and seek to grow the business elsewhere.

Given how consistently these policies have underperformed expectations, there has been an ongoing concern that UNM could need to inject more capital to support future claims. By reinsuring 19% of the business and doing so in an accretive way, the likelihood that UNM will need to provide additional support is much lower. With this transaction, investors can focus increasingly on the rest of the business and less on LTC.

I Value UNM Excluding the Closed Block's Equity

Still, this will be an ongoing and gradual process. Of its $11.2 billion in book equity, $3.6 billion sits in the closed block. The closed block has the majority of the assets, at $33 billion out of $62 billion. Given this transaction closed on July 1st, we should see a step-down in Closed Block exposure in Q3 results. My underlying assumption continues to be that the equity in the Closed Block has no value for UNM shareholders.

Essentially, I expect that equity to be needed to support ongoing claims. To the extent that UNM has excess equity it can eventually withdraw, it is likely years away given how long-term LTC contracts are. Importantly, I do not see UNM needing to inject more capital to support these policies, and this deal makes that an even more remote risk. Still, I would not assign any value to the unit.

This is important because such a substantial portion of Unum's consolidated book value includes the Closed Block. Unum has a $76 book value excluding accumulated other comprehensive income. However, excluding the closed block and unrealized losses, book value stands at $49. I view $49 as the more relevant book value metrics, since it is the rest of the business that ultimately is supporting UNM's dividend and buybacks.

Q1 Was Mixed, but Issues Should Be Temporary

While this is a significant movement that helps Unum move beyond its LTC challenges, I would note that recent performance in the remaining "core" insurance business has been a bit more mixed. In the company's first quarter reported on April 29th, Unum earned $2.04, which missed expectations by $0.15 as premiums grew by just under 4% to $2.7 billion. Colonial Life was a bright spot as it earned $116 million with sales rising by 2%.

The company faced two primary challenges. First, claims were higher than last year. Policy benefits rose by $116 million, whereas premiums grew by just $92 million. As a result, Unum US earnings declined by 15% to $329 million. Premiums grew by 4%, but disability benefits rose to 61.8% from 57.5% last year. Last year's underwriting was extremely strong, which created some of the unfavorability. Management targets a low-60% benefit ratio, so this outcome was still consistent with targets.

Beyond the normalization in underwriting, net investment income was stable at $513 million. The earned book yield fell to 4.25% from 4.44%, which was a surprisingly large decline, given the reinvestment environment remains favorable. UNM's portfolio is fairly conservative. It includes $35.8 billion of fixed income $1.7 billion of alternatives. As you can see, the fixed income portfolio is fairly conservative, with 96% rated investment grade.

However, its alternatives portfolio delivered poor results. Alts earned 5.1%, and as noted on the earnings call, Q1 results can be seasonally weaker given delays in getting 12/31 valuations. Notably, even in late April when the stock market was substantially lower, management reiterated its 8-10% return expectation. With asset valuations substantially higher today, this guidance is even more credible, and I expect to see alternative returns improve meaningfully in Q2 and Q3.

UNM is Attractive Given Strong Capital Returns and Attractive Multiple

Importantly, Unum is very well capitalized. Risk-based capital ("RBC") is now at 460%, well above the 400-425% I like to see. Unum also has $2.2 billion of holding company liquidity. As such, UNM can aggressively return capital to investors. On May 22nd, Unum announced it will raise its dividend by nearly 10% to $0.46, starting in the third quarter. That gives shares a 2.3% go-forward dividend yield. It also did $200 million of Q1 buybacks, and as a result, its share count is down over 7% from last year. We should see at least $200 million of buybacks in Q2.

Back in March, I was targeting $1.3 billion of capital returns and $9 of EPS. Depending on the speed of share repurchases, I expect capital returns to come in at $1.2-$1.3 billion. I also expect much of the Q1 miss to prove transitory given the likely rebound in alternatives income, which should result in ~$9.00 of EPS. In theory, with a 20% core ROE, UNM could trade 2x book value and still provide a 10% return for investors, suggesting upside into the upper $90's, considering my $49 adjusted book value.

In practice, I believe a lower price target is appropriate. While the overhang from LTC is diminishing thanks in part to this week's announcement, there clearly is still some overhang and risk associated with these policies. I also expect it reinsured some of the better policies, with more toxic exposure still being retained. While most insurers are trading in the 11-13x zone, I continue to view about 10x earnings as more reasonable.

That still points to an upside of about 11% to $90, and with UNM repurchasing 6-7% of its shares each year, there continues to be technical support for shares. With a single-digit multiple, I view these buybacks as accretive, and they will support meaningful EPS growth. With Closed Block pressure declining, UNM should enjoy ongoing, but gradual multiple expansion as the overhang diminishes, and I view shares as attractive at 9x earnings.

data.title: UnitedHealth: My Uncomfortable Buy With Possible Upside Despite All The Red Flags
data.description:
UnitedHealth Group (NYSE:UNH) is currently one of the most difficult stocks to price and, consequently, to assign a specific rating. It is a time when a solid company with a business model that was previously known for its resilience is facing many problems at once, which may be both cyclical and structural.

To be honest, I don't like UNH as a long-term investment, even though this is my focus as an investor. The sector and the company as a whole seem to me to have unnecessary complexity for most portfolios and investors. It is very dependent on politicians and regulators, and the risks/uncertainties could not be greater at the moment.

And yet, there's a catch. UNH stock has fundamentals that are actually quite good, but the stock is being punished so severely by the market that any minimally good news could be "great" and conservative estimates still point to a very cheap valuation. For this reason, practically this reason alone, my rating here is a 'hesitant' or "uncomfortable" buy if you will.

UnitedHealth Group: Endless Problems

Before even moving on to the valuation, I believe it is essential to explain why UNH is an uncomfortable buy for me. It is essential to understand the problems and uncertainties. To begin with, it is difficult to find a coherent or didactic way to explain all the problems, since it is not just one headwind, but several problems at once.

I'll start with soaring medical costs, which are reducing profitability. On the earnings call, the CEO explained this very succinctly. The company expected care activity in 2025 to increase at a consistent rate, but in fact, the increase was twice that rate.

And as the chart below shows, UNH's revenue trend couldn't be better, but an essential component for value creation is margin. Note that this margin is already small, so it would be pointless if the prospects pointed to revenue that looked like a compounder but a margin that was increasingly compressed.

A catalyst for this financial uncertainty is leadership, with the "sudden departure" of the CEO. If that weren't enough, not long ago, the company began to be investigated by the DoJ in connection with Medicare Fraud. Just the existence of this investigation in the midst of all these problems, and for a company as important as UNH (which has a very significant market share), is very bad for the company, shareholders, and the stock.

Finally, an investigation by The Guardian alleged that UNH is making secret payments to nursing homes. I won't even go into the merits of the likelihood of this, but what is correct to say is that regardless of the level of truth or the level at which this is occurring, it is already very damaging to the company's reputation.

In short, UNH faces operational/financial problems. It faces governance issues. It faces problems related to its image/reputation. It is difficult to imagine a more uncertain situation than this. Not to mention other problems that are not just circumstantial, such as heavy regulation.

Why UNH is My Hesitant Buy

Yes, these are problems that seem to have no end, so why would any investor expose themselves to them? Well, the answer is price. The market has already reacted strongly to this news and knocked the stock down from over $600 to under $300.

UnitedHealth is now trading at a forward P/E of 14.7x, while the 5-year avg. is around 20.5x, i.e., a discount of almost 30%. To be honest, a 30% discount doesn't sound like much considering everything that's going on, but there's a reason why it's not. This discount is already taking into account a 20% drop in EPS in 2025, which means that under "normal" conditions, the company has the capacity to deliver a much higher net income.

Taking the estimates for 2027, for example, the market already assumes an EPS of $30, slightly above what was delivered in 2024. In this scenario, which seems very plausible if the operational and financial problems stabilize, the P/E would be 10.7x.

Remember that even with all these problems, the company still has the characteristics of a compounder. Its revenue, for example, has shown a CAGR of 10.5% over the last 5 years. With a stable margin, a price-to-earnings of 10x is definitely not the "ideal" level (I'd say it's a bargain), in fact, it's more like something close to 25x, perhaps a little less due to discounts from recent uncertainties, but it's still a considerable upside, with a potential of 100% in a few years.

In addition, from a technical analysis point of view (I'm no expert on this, I must make it clear), the stock no longer looks like a falling knife, it's already setting up some base, and it has some important resistance points that it could eventually seek out, such as ~$340 and ~$400.

The Bottom Line

UnitedHealth Group is facing one of the most challenging environments I know. It's not like luxury companies like LVMH (OTCPK:LVMHF) (OTCPK:LVMUY) and Prada (OTCPK:PRDSF) (OTCPK:PRDSY) are facing a slowdown in industry-wide revenue or anything like that. There are numerous relevant problems and complications that may persist for at least a few quarters until the market again gives the benefit of the doubt.

And yet, it seems to me that the asymmetry is positive. In the event of a normalization of the scenario, which even if it takes a while, seems plausible to me, there is a tremendous upside here, potentially 100% without making too optimistic calculations.

Even so, it's worth mentioning that this is not a "buy and hold" rating, but rather a more valuation-driven buy rating due to the upside potential, with a slightly more speculative nature that requires greater caution.

data.title: CIHEX: Capturing Upside In A Rising Market
data.description:
By Jason Hill

Participating in a Strong Market Rebound

Calamos Hedged Equity Fund (CIHEX) is designed as an equity alternative — seeking to participate in market upside while limiting exposure to drawdowns. During the second quarter, CIHEX earned 6.70%, capturing over 60% of the S&P 500 Index’s 10.94% gain. We believe this underscores the strength of our asymmetric strategy in rising markets, as the fund advanced while still preserving the risk-mitigation characteristics that served investors well during the volatility of the first quarter.

Equity markets rebounded sharply during the second quarter, supported by resilient corporate earnings, optimism about deregulation, and continued momentum in AI innovation. This environment — marked by steady gains and reduced volatility — created favorable conditions for our strategy to demonstrate its upside participation capabilities.

Leveraging Our Asymmetric Profile

Our core hedge structure, implemented in late 2023, was designed to potentially capture approximately 65% of the market’s upside while limiting downside exposure to 35% over a one-year horizon. As the equity market advanced in the second quarter, this structure performed as intended and allowed the fund to participate in the majority of the market gains while maintaining a defensive stance.

We believe this asymmetric profile is central to why investors choose CIHEX: to maintain meaningful equity exposure across diverse market environments, with a disciplined approach to risk management.

Strategic Hedge Enhancement

As active managers, we took advantage of favorable market conditions at the end of June to roll our hedge out to June 2026 and adjust strike levels upward. This tactical move:

  • Enhances upside participation potential for the coming year,
  • Reduces the cost of theta bleed* (i.e., increased option value decline as expiration approaches), and
  • Preserves our commitment to downside risk mitigation.

Importantly, this adjustment is also designed to preserve our core 65% upside / 35% downside risk-reward profile, ensuring continuity in our strategic positioning. This strategic adjustment reflects our ability to respond dynamically to market opportunities while maintaining the integrity of our risk-managed framework.

Looking Ahead

The first half of 2025 has delivered both significant volatility and meaningful market gains — highlighting the importance of maintaining a balanced, flexible approach. We believe CIHEX’s performance across both Q1’s challenges and Q2’s strength exemplifies our strategy’s value: capturing upside while limiting downside.

As we move into the second half of the year, our enhanced hedge positioning and active management approach position the fund to continue delivering on its core objective — pursuing meaningful equity market participation with significantly reduced downside exposure.

CIHEX: A Time-Tested Active Approach to Capturing Upside, While Managing Downside Volatility

Total Returns as of 06/30/25

2Q 2025 1 Year 3 Years 5 Years 10 Years Fund Inception
CIHEX 6.70% 10.31% 12.51% 8.44% 7.32% 7.04% (12/31/14)
S&P 500 Index 10.94% 15.16% 19.70% 16.64% 13.64% 13.10%

Source: Morningstar. Performance data quoted represents past performance, which is no guarantee of future results. Current performance may be lower or higher than the performance quoted. Please refer to Important Risk Information. The principal value and return of an investment will fluctuate so that your shares, when redeemed, may be worth more or less than their original cost. Performance reflected at NAV does not include the Fund’s maximum front-end sales load of 4.75%. Had it been included, the Fund’s return would have been lower. The fund’s gross expense ratio as of the prospectus dated 2/28/2025 is 0.90% for Class I shares.

Average annual total return measures net investment income and capital gain or loss from portfolio investments as an annualized average. All performance shown assumes reinvestment of dividends and capital gains distributions.

*Theta measures the rate of decline in the value of an option over time.

Original Post

data.title: I Love Meta Platforms As A User, But As An Investor, I Feel Antsy... (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
My parents warned me about marrying outside the religion.

Diluting the faith was a concern for them. And it was largely so or their generation.

But that wasn’t all.

They understood I wasn’t conventionally religious. They warned me that a wife who converts might wind up more devout than I… and pressured me to step it up.

Amazingly, I note some empirical support for their “concerns.”

There really is a phenomenon known as zeal of the convert. It refers to “the very fervent devotion to new beliefs, which are completely different from one's old beliefs.”

Go figure.

It usually describes religion… For example, a UK study showed that “51% of converts worship at least once a week, compared to 44% of non-converts.”

Does this phenomenon also apply outside of religious conversion?

I can’t speak for society as a whole. But I can say it applied to me.

I’ve been a long-time Facebook hater. But as I admitted in my 6/22/24 Meta Platforms (NASDAQ:META) write-up, I came to see the light.

If it were as simple as keeping tabs on childhood friends or seeing pictures of my cousins’ countless grandchildren or old friend’s vacations, no thanks. Z-z-z-z-z-z!

I learned it’s so, so, so much more. Just look me up on Facebook and check out the number and variety of Groups to which I belong.

Some or all my them may not interest you personally. But I’ve little doubt you could find plenty of others more aligned top your interests.

Just don’t rush to delete seeming junk email group suggestions Facebook sends you. Maybe it's their AI. Maybe not. Either way, they do a good job of targeting what individual users are likely to want to see.

And once Instagram’s algorithm learns what you like and dislike, you’ll find it, too, can delight you for as many hours as you dare spend scrolling. It does so for my regular grownup-business oriented account. And it does so for what I refer to as my trashy stupidity-oriented finsta account.

And I have to think Meta likes me too. Noting the incredible relevance of the ads they show me, I look at them, click occasionally, and sometimes buy.

Win-win… How great is this!

Anyway, the stock rewarded my conversion. Since I said “Buy,” it rose 44%. The S&P 500 is up too, but not that much. It rose 14%.

I’m Not Denying There are Parts of Meta That Don’t Grab Me

If you can tell me why Threads continues to exist, please go in the comment section and enlighten me. I’m currently clueless.

(Even if it’s nonsense, I can live with it. Amazon.com (AMZN) blundered badly when it tried to offer a mobile phone. I’m inclined to put Threads in that category… a mess from a company big and strong enough to absorb and move beyond it.)

Meanwhile, I express no personal opinion about Messenger (formerly Facebook Messenger) or WhatsApp.

For those who don’t know, the latter is a messaging sort-of-texting type service. But it uses an internet protocol rather than the more familiar (to many of us) SMS protocol.

Personally, I’m fine with regular text.

But many appreciate WhatsApp’s richer media capabilities. And it’s huge in much of the world outside the U.S. (My wife, who is Chinese and does business with many from South America, swears by it.)

And here, too, it looks like it’s becoming a win-win proposition that benefits both users and Meta. Meta is introducing advertising into WhatsApp. Having done so well here with Facebook and Instagram, one has to be optimistic about the prospects for WhatsApp ads.

In Terms of Fundamental Analysis, This Company Remains Excellent

Here are the trends in quarterly Revenues and EPS

Let’s go to \the elephant in the room… or in the EPS table.

2022 was a rotten year.

The advertising business, META’s main operation, felt the impact of rising interest rates, inflation, and recession fears. These restrained ad spending. Worse still, Apple added privacy features that made it harder for advertisers to track user behavior.

We’ve moved past that.

But there’s another issue… Reality Labs. That’s META’s fancy name for its activities relating to visionary tech things. The company spent heavily on that.

I’ll discuss it further below as a huge source of concern.

For now, though, let’s look at how strong META has been and still is fundamentally… even with Reality Labs continuing to exist

The following tables compare META, GOOGL, companies I drew (using the Seeking Alpha Equity Screener) from among those in Consumer Discretionary and Staples sectors. I chose those I think likely to advertise heavily to consumers. I also show the companies in the SPDR S&P 500 ETF (SPY).

(I prefer medians since these aren’t impacted by wild distortions often caused by unusual data items, even in big companies that can dominate weighted averages.)

Let’s start with historical and projected forward growth rates respectively.

GOOGL, which also harbors beyond-advertising tech dreams, attracts a lot of love from analysts. But META’s growth profile is better.

Here are the core fundamentals. I say “core” because these numbers tend to be more durable… i.e. not battered around by short-term things.

Again, META and GOOGL look to be fundamental powerhouses.

If you want to get fussy, you can give GOOGL a slight nod. Debt plays a lesser role on its balance sheet. But with an 86.90 interest coverage ratio, let’s not shed tears for META.

If Shakespeare could reincarnate into current times, I’d suggest he write a play called “Meta and Google.” They may not be in love like Romeo and Juliet. But a new play would involve corporate angst worthy of the Bard’s attention.

You see, META and GOOGL have both mastered the art and science of 21st century (digital) advertising. Yet, both have been choosing to focus their hearts… and money… elsewhere.

I’ve expressed concerns about GOOGL here and here.

My first META impression during my current Seeking Alpha incarnation was positive. It seems to be doing a great job using AI to enhance its core competency. Lately, though, I’ve been wondering about its wandering eye (and the dollars that are following)…

Real Thinking About Reality Labs

META tells us on page 7 of the latest 10K that…

Many of our Reality Labs investments are directed toward long-term, cutting edge research and development for products that are not on the market today and may only be fully realized in the next decade.

That sounds a lot like GOOGL’s “Other Bets.” Pages 4 and 6 of the latter’s 10K refer to those as “non-Google businesses” and “Moonshots.”

Waymo-love may be helping the street view GOOGL’s “Other Bets” more favorably than I do. (See, e.g. here.) My general impression of META, though, is that many on the Street are less pleased with its Reality Labs. (See, e.g., here.)

Why the difference?

That’s hard to say. Perhaps it has to do with Mark Zuckerberg, the face of META and, of course, its CEO, having become embroiled in more sensitive controversies such as difficult content, privacy issues, etc. Arguably, GOOGL’s controversies seem more traditional business-like, such as search competition, and antitrust issues.

That may be some of it. But I think a lot more has to do with GOOGL starting to show something tangible for its moonshots.

I’m not as convinced as many that Waymo can beat Tesla (TSLA) long term in robo-taxi. But at least people in some parts of the country people can now sit in and ride the result of GOOGL’s Waymo moonshot. And they can do so at a more every-person price than what’s so far been needed to go along with META’s dreams.

As many know, Reality Labs is now mainly centered around virtual reality (VR).

A while back, it was something the company called the metaverse. This company, formerly known as Facebook (FB), started pushing this idea in 2021… even rebranding itself to what we now refer to as Meta Platforms (META).

The Metaverse was supposed to have been a fully immersive environment. It was supposed to have redefined notions of what the internet is and could be.

I read the descriptions and watched an on-line video in which Zuckerberg demonstrated the metaverse. I thought it was ridiculous.

It struck me as an overblown rehash of something Reuters was enthusiastic about when I worked there in the 2000s. It was called Second Life.

Like many at Reuters, I registered and created an avatar. I entered the environment. And I couldn’t think of a single thing I was even remotely tempted to do. I abandoned that nonsense.

I got a sense Reuters thought it would sell advertising within its Second-Life headquarters or lounge or whatever else it was supposed to have been. I’m not sure it ever made a penny from that.

It was an intriguing curiosity. According to a 5/8/24 blog:

[A]lthough it boasts 70 million registered users, Second Life’s daily user headcount in 2023 clocked in at less than 0.3 percent of that figure. Its active user base peaked in 2010, and it’s been in steady decline since 2017, with newer, flashier rivals scooping up much of Second Life’s younger demographic.

Being as kind as I can to the Metaverse, I’ll say it’s one of the “flashier rivals.” But, it seems, flashier hasn’t been good enough:

‘The metaverse has changed from an inevitable digital frontier to a niche, experimental space with uncertain viability. It’s not the fully immersive digital utopia that was promised,’ (Thomas Franklin, founder and CEO of Swapped.com) says. ‘A few years ago, companies threw billions into virtual spaces, betting on a massive shift in how people interact online. That shift never fully materialized.’

For proof, Franklin notes that Meta’s Horizon Worlds struggled to retain even 200,000 users out of Facebook’s total 3 billion, while Decentraland and the Sandbox, once seen as virtual real estate gold mines, now attract daily users in the low thousands. Companies poured millions into virtual stores, events, and non-fungible tokens (NFTs)—the unique digital assets that represent ownership of real-world or digital items—only to realize most consumers never showed up. JPMorgan, for example, predicted a $1 trillion metaverse economy when it launched a lounge in Decentraland in 2022. A year later, the platform averaged fewer than 5,000 daily users.

But even when users show up, profits haven’t followed. “’Brands quickly realized that digital foot traffic does not equal real-world sales,’ Franklin continues.

Wall Street sentient toward the Metaverse migrated from early enthusiasm to eventual negativity:

Despite Zuckerberg’s vision of a virtual world enhancing user engagement, investor sentiment has soured on these costly experiments. The Globe and Mail highlights that Meta’s share price struggles partly stem from this unprofitable venture, as the company balances its social media dominance with unproven bets.

Today, these “costly experiments” have shifted mainly from the Metaverse to VR.

What exactly is VR? Meta says:

Virtual reality is a computer-generated simulation of a 3D environment that you can explore as part of a fully immersive experience. You’re represented in this world as an avatar and using a VR headset and handheld controllers, you can interact with the people and objects you find there.

The link inside the above quote is part of Meta’s original presentation. If you click on it, you’ll get more expansive definitions.

That same Meta resource that supplied the VR quote also gives a definition of the Metaverse:

Accessed through VR, AR and smart glasses, the metaverse is a collective virtual space that brings together 2D and 3D digital environments, social interactions, and experiences that go beyond individual platforms or applications.

So how different are the two – in real-people language? Darned if I know!

I suppose Metaverse was about big goofy-looking goggles like Oculus while VR is about cooler more stylish glasses.

Now don’t hold me to this… I’m just guessing. And yes, I can see where my observation may not fit squarely with the two Meta quotations I just supplied.

I’m just working from penumbras and emanations flowing from the last two block quotes, indications that META is focusing less on VR hardware (supposedly, goggles) and more on glasses, and that it’s shifting from Metaverse to VR.

That’s a mouthful. And it’s a darned expensive mouthful. Check out the following segment information. Notice how much money META is willing to keep losing on Reality Labs!

Huge negative margins such as those I show for Reality Labs might arguably be brushed off with NM (“Not Meaningful”) labels. I won’t do that. META is so deliberate regarding its Reality Labs projects I have to say the margins are very meaningful.

Meanwhile, is VR likely to please users any more than turned out to have been the case with the Metaverse?

Apple (AAPL) hasn’t exactly impressed many (any?) with its spatial computing venture, which to me seems very VR like.

Is that a signal of potential user opinion of VR? Will cooler glasses as opposed to bulky goggles be a difference maker? Will META’s glasses even fare well competitively against others jumping into VR glasses?

I don’t know. But the burden of proof right now remains on META.

So far, its track record when it comes to judging how users have been reacting to what Reality Labs has been churning out has not been good. So I’m not feeling confident.

To the extent consumers have been surveyed, it seems content is important to them. They’ll adopt the new stuff if (costs drop and) related content makes it worthwhile.

So far, though, it looks to me, a META observer, like the company is all in on fancy tech and completely oblivious to creating or arranging for worthy content.

In all fairness, I heard such no-content arguments when new video game consoles were being introduced way back in the industry’s early days. Content eventually came. So it may yet come to VR. So ultimately…

Who the heck knows?

  • I can’t end this section without addressing META’s AI efforts… So, here goes.

As I mentioned above and on 6/22/24, it looks like META is doing an excellent job in using AI to make its advertising businesses a lot better.

I certainly have no quarrel with that. And I look forward to seeing how much more the company accomplishes along these lines going forward.

But… (You knew there was going to be a “but” coming along, didn’t you!)

It’s one thing to create a tech capability for one’s own use. It’s quite another to do that with the idea of selling to the world at large.

The latter is harder… much, much harder. You can’t just satisfy your own needs. You also have to anticipate the needs and goals of countless others you haven’t met or communicated with.

Has META shown promise in developing AI for sale to who-knows-how-many-currently-unknown users who want to do who-knows-how-many-different-kinds-of-things? One source says it’s not happening:

Metas push into artificial intelligence has long been framed as a race to catch up with rivals like Microsoft, Google, and OpenAI. But new benchmarks and market data reveal a troubling reality: the company is not just trailing its competitors—it is falling further behind in nearly every critical category, from market share to model performance to strategic infrastructure investments.

Obviously, others may argue to the contrary. Permit me, however, to phrase the matter differently…

Does the world need a general-use AI model from Meta?

I’m sure if I visited META’s headquarters and asked that question, things would go badly. But seriously… How many AI vendors does the world need beyond what it already has?

Clearly, there will always be room for others that demonstrate some special proficiency unmatched by existing providers. But is META doing that?

Investors could decide by studying up on deep-tech stuff. (That goes right over my head. If you can handle it, I tip my hat!) Or we’ll have to watch future market share numbers… if we feel like watching Reality Labs to continue pouring red ink while all that’s going on.

And that brings me to the gazillion-dollar question….

Does META -- As It Now Is -- Still Make Sense as a Publicly Traded Corporate Entity?

If you peruse the Analysis portion of my Profile, you’ll see I’m very supportive of companies devoid or nearly devoid of revenues and profits but long on vision.

In my 6/28/25 Coinbase (COIN) writeup, I went so far as to say:

Many believe teens and 20-somethings can better use new tech. I think that’s an open question. But we 60 and 70 or more somethings have seen and experienced a lot. Don’t underestimate our ability to visualize future potential! Perhaps that’s why, as I aged, I became more of a growth investor.

On 6/10/25, I said “Buy” for QuantumScape (QS) advocating an angel investor mindset. And that came even without yet knowing if the new battery QS is developing will ever work. (The stock has since popped in response to good news on that front. But that’s beside the point for today.)

So, why I’ve been bashing Reality Labs. Wouldn’t it seem to fall right in my wheelhouse?

Maybe.

I don’t love everything new. I still have to believe in the aims and see indications of potential to execute. Maybe if I could separate Reality Labs from Meta and do a deeper dive on just that, I might like it.

And THAT is the point!

I can’t separate Reality Labs from META. And therein lies the problem. Those who own shares of COIN, or QS, or any of the quantum computing stocks I wrote up early this year CHOSE to do so.

They CHOSE to pursue visionary futuristic opportunities. And they CHOSE to assume the associated risks.

META shareholders are being forced to deal with Reality Labs whether they want to or not.

I don’t like that!

Risks

Let’s start with the usuals…. Potential cyclical weakness in ad spending, potential disappointments if spending to beef up digital advertising AI runs ahead of quarterly revenue expectations, the fact that META is still in Reality Labs and the possibility the Street may come to hate that more.

And for as much as the market liked META’s recent results, it hasn’t been that long since investors punished the stock for disappointing results and guidance. Each new quarter poses a new risk of disappointment regarding risk and/or guidance.

I also wonder if management’s attention to Reality Labs is distracting it from competitive threats. I love META properties. But I’m a 70-something Boomer.

TikTok seems to be leading the way with Gen Z. That’s the future. META needs to focus on what most matters.

If profitable offerings are ever going to come out of Reality Labs, then META needs to get more serious than it’s shown itself to be on the content front.

And let’s not forget the way META seems like a fountain of lawyers’ billable hours.

My head spins just thinking of all the regulatory controversies around privacy, content, moderation or lack thereof, and all things involving minors and other vulnerable persons.

And of course, who knows how the judge hearing an antitrust case advocating the breakup of META’s collection of ad-supported social media properties will decide (probably later this year).

META offers defenses that sound pretty good to me. But my opinion plus one hundred cents will buy you a one dollar bill. We’ll just have to wait for the judge, the one who matters, to rule.

It could go badly for META shareholders is the company loses. Of course, if META gets hit with a bad ruling, expect a long and serious trip through the appellate process.

What to do About META Stock

Let’s start with the basics. Here are the valuations.

These look a bit on the high side. I’d pin that on the Reality Labs vision angle. But comparisons with GOOGL call that into question. The latter’s metrics reflect in part ifs own venture into futurism (“Other Bets”). And they’re leaner than those of META.

Here’s the price chart.

I see no problems here at this time.

The price, the 10-day exponential moving average (EMA) and the 50-day EMA are configured beautifully… I mean bullishly.

Consider, too, Chaikin Money Flow (CMF) and the Chaikin Oscillator (CO). Both measure which party to trades is more motivated. CMF does it for institutional investors. CO does it for the market in general.

The CMF isn’t at peak strength. But it’s not presently threatening to go meaningfully bearish. CO is OK. Neither suggests near-term pressure on META stock.

When it comes to rating stocks, I think in terms of probable future stock performance relative to the market, rather than literally buying, holding or selling.

And since I’m not rating based on a quant model, I’ll eschew “Strong …” extremes. Absent an exceptional degree of conviction, I think “Buy,” Hold,” or “Sell” are enough.

There’s much to love about META. That’s why I said “Buy” back on 6/22/24.

There’s enough META-love in the market to suggest it won’t likely underperform in the foreseeable future.

But looking ahead, considering the risks, and the Reality Labs quagmire, I think it’s most appropriate that I restrain my former enthusiasm for the stock.

I now see META as a likely average performer relative to the broader market.

I’d probably change my mind if I thought META might do a spinoff, or something like that with Reality Labs.

If I were a super-hotshot investment banker, I’d probably try to generate deals in which Reality Labs and possibly GOOGL’s Other Bets collection come together as an independent company. I might even propose some deals with AAPL regarding spatial computing and AI.

But I’m not a power-banker. So, let’s shove that idea to the side.

Ultimately, I translate my average-performer view to the Seeking Alpha rating taxonomy by now downgrading my META rating to “HOLD.

data.title: Snapchat: Margin Expansion Will Power The Snap-Back
data.description:
Snapchat (SNAP) might be in trouble. That certainly is what’s implied by the low stock valuation, which places the stock at a deep discount to close peers. The company is not as profitable and its balance sheet is more leveraged than peers. The company appears to be affected by tariffs disproportionately more than peers, further adding to the downward pressure on the stock price. That said, the stock looks cheap here, assuming management can eventually lift margins in the long term. I reiterate my buy rating but caution on likely volatility.

SNAP Stock Price

I last covered SNAP in January, where I upgraded the stock due to the lagging performance. The stock has struggled, declining by 20% as execution wavers.

There are a lot of “value trap” signs here, but the valuation nonetheless looks too cheap to ignore.

SNAP Stock Key Metrics

SNAP is a social media company which has struggled in recent years due to escalating competition from Meta Platforms (META) and TikTok. In the most recent quarter, SNAP saw North America users finally decline after stagnating for many quarters.

That is a troubling sign, especially considering that North American average revenue per user ('ARPU') stands far higher than the rest. The company is seeing its most lucrative users struggle to grow, and the market has noticed.

The company nonetheless still saw revenues jump 14% YoY in the quarter to $1.363 billion, exceeding guidance of between $1.325 billion to $1.36 billion.

Management has sought to offset the slowing topline growth with margin expansion. Adjusted EBITDA margins jumped 400 bps YoY.

The company ended the quarter with $3.2 billion of cash versus $3.6 billion in debt, representing one of the weakest balance sheets among peers. The company’s lack of historic profitability has prevented it from enjoying the same net cash positions as the competition. I note that the company refinanced $1.5 billion of convertible notes with 6.875% notes maturing in 2033, which will lead to higher interest expenses moving forward.

On the conference call, management noted that they were not giving guidance for the second quarter due to seeing “some headwinds.” There are many alternate universes in which this would have been forgiven due to the understandable macro uncertainty, but social media peers like Meta Platforms (META) and Pinterest (PINS) did not report the same weakness. That said, I found it notable that management appeared to try to reframe the narrative to focus on profitability, with them saying that they are “well-positioned to deliver improved business performance and meaningful positive free cash flow as we make further progress towards GAAP profitability.” It is not promising for the growth story for management to already be pulling the profitability levers, but it makes sense with the stock trading where it is.

Is SNAP Stock A Buy, Sell, Or Hold?

As of recent prices, SNAP traded hands at just around 2.7x sales. That represents a steep discount to the 6x and 10x multiples at PINS and META, respectively. Consensus estimates look too optimistic, as they imply an acceleration in growth moving forward.

Between the overly optimistic consensus estimates and the lack of meaningful profitability, it might be tempting to just cast SNAP aside and move on. But I am of the view that the company should be able to achieve significantly higher normalized margins, perhaps at least in the 25% range. That would place the stock trading hands at just around 10.8x long-term earnings. SNAP is no META, but I can see long-term multiples settling at least 12x to 15x earnings (down from my prior estimate of 18x earnings). Sure, SNAP’s balance sheet is not as strong as peers, but the leverage ratio is still arguably quite modest relative to the broader market. If SNAP can sustain around 6% to 8% top-line growth moving forward, then I can see the stock delivering market-beating returns as it executes against margin expansion.

SNAP Stock Risks

SNAP might be facing a negative feedback loop. If user growth continues to be negative, then the appeal of the social network may fade. The company’s lack of material profitability constrains its ability to invest to fight that trend. It is unclear if the damage can be repaired or if it is too late. The stock looks cheap assuming margins can be driven higher, but it is not clear if management will be able to drive that expansion.

SNAP Stock Conclusion

I am of the view that SNAP is finding a valuation bottom, as the low price to sales multiple looks conservative enough to offset the numerous risks. The company is not handling macro headwinds in stride like peers and is still ways off GAAP profitability. Management appears to understand the task, and I expect future margin expansion to be an important catalyst for a multiple re-rating. I reiterate my buy rating for the stock.

data.title: U.S. Equities: Room To Run, But Selectivity Matters More
data.description:
By John Hillenbrand, CPA

The first half of 2025 was a remarkable testament to the resilience and adaptability of the US economy and equity markets. The markets experienced a brief but notably sharp reaction to the President’s tariff plans, declining almost 20% from first quarter highs, but quickly rebounded in a classic “V-shaped” recovery. While details about tax and trade policies remain limited, investors were encouraged by indications that worst-case scenarios were being taken off the table.

Importantly, investors are now positioned to gain much-needed clarity on several key policies, including tariff implementation, the “One Big Beautiful Bill” working its way through Congress, and the future direction of Fed policy.

Even as equity markets have begun pricing in positive outcomes to these uncertainties, investors should remember that immediate success is not guaranteed, and the sharp rebound in equity valuations leaves little room for missteps.

That said, we believe favorable resolutions to tariff and tax policies will dramatically reduce uncertainty and allow companies and investors to adjust with confidence to the new landscape. This should provide sufficient positive momentum to keep equity valuations elevated in the near term.

Although the clock is ticking on the 90-day tariff pause set to expire July 9, we believe the most extreme reciprocal tariffs are unlikely to be fully implemented (with the possible exception of China). This should afford companies additional time to adjust their supply chains, ultimately reducing downside risk to corporate earnings.

Despite conflicting headlines, we expect eventual passage of the tax bill, which should provide incremental benefits to workers' take-home pay and help sustain consumer spending. Simultaneously, the bill should materially reduce regulatory burdens through the "10-for-1" deregulation protocol and provide a framework for long-term productivity growth.

Finally, we believe slowing inflation trends will make it increasingly difficult for the Federal Reserve to continue delaying additional rate cuts. Historically, rate cuts coupled with a healthy job market, solid corporate balance sheets, manageable energy costs, and continued consumer spending have provided an attractive fundamental backdrop for US equity markets.

From a market perspective, with the S&P 500 Index back near all-time highs, we are no longer in recovery mode where “a rising tide lifts all boats.” This makes individual security selection increasingly important for the remainder of the year.

Fortunately, Calamos Growth Fund’s all-cap strategy provides us with ample opportunities to access growth across market caps and sectors. We maintain significant exposure to the Magnificent 7 stocks but are also continuing to add to emerging companies in transformative themes such as cybersecurity, robotics, immunotherapy, and agentic AI.

While we acknowledge that the economy may still encounter short-term turbulence as trade and tax agreements progress — with inevitable starts and stops along the way — we believe the fundamentals of the US economy remain strong. We view any drawdowns as offering long-term investors tactical opportunities to deploy capital among high-quality companies.

Overall, we maintain our positive bias toward growth equities. We will continue focusing on being well-compensated for the risks we undertake and believe this discipline will help generate strong risk-adjusted returns.

Original Post

data.title: U.S. To Scrap Proposed Tax Rule That Could Have Targeted Canadian Investors
data.description:
Originally published on June 27, 2025

The U.S. Treasury Secretary has announced an agreement with lawmakers to scrap a controversial proposal in the so-called “One Big Beautiful Bill” that could have resulted in Canadians paying higher withholding tax on dividends from U.S. investments. Nicole Ewing, Principal, Wealth Planning Office, TD Wealth, joins MoneyTalk to break down the latest developments and what it could mean for investors.

Transcript

Greg Bonnell: In a bit of a twist out of Washington, Section 899, known as the "revenge tax," has been pulled from that One Big Beautiful Bill Act. The move reverses what could have been a costly tax change for Canadian investors. So, what now? Nicole Ewing, Principal Wealth Planning Office, TD Wealth, joins us with a look at the implications.

Nicole, always great to have you with us.

Nicole Ewing: Great to be here.

Greg Bonnell: You've been following the story very carefully. We spoke to you on several occasions just in the past two weeks about this. So we have this big development right now. What does this potentially mean when we talk about tax exposure for Canadian investors?

Nicole Ewing: So this was a recommendation from the US Treasury Secretary to scrap this. It was accepted and followed through by the US Congressional Joint Committee on Taxation. So that's both the House and the Senate. And they agreed to pull this. And what it essentially does is puts us in the status quo situation that we're currently in.

So what that is for Canadians who are investing in US-- of US exposure through their investments would be-- so generally speaking, there's a 30% tax withholding that would apply. But by treaty, that's reduced to 15%. That's dividend income.

And it's reduced to 15% in your non-registered account in your TFSA, FHSA, RESP. In your retirement fund-- so your registered retirement, RRSP, LIF, LIRA, RRIF-- it's reduced to 0%. What this would have done is increased that by 20%, maybe more, depending on how the rule is going to play it out. But we're going to keep the status quo there.

With respect to real estate, there was potential that it would be increasing US real estate held by Canadians who are disposing of that property, currently 15% withholding. That, potentially, would have gone up to 35%. That's also going to be status quo at 15%.

So, all to say, current rules will continue to apply. We won't have that shock of an increase of tax that people were really concerned about.

Greg Bonnell: So even though we're at status quo, you talked about dividend treatment, capital gains impacts. Is there still reason to pay attention here?

Nicole Ewing: I would suggest there is. So whenever you are invested in foreign investments and you have that exposure, it's important to be mindful of the fact that we don't know what's going to happen, either by foreign governments, foreign circumstances that may happen, whether it's the US or otherwise. So we want to be mindful of our comfort with that.

So if this shook you up, if this made you think, "Oh, dear, this is going to have a really big impact on me"-- this would be an opportunity to reflect and think, am I appropriately diversified? Is my risk tolerance still what it was when I set up my holdings? And so, really thinking about if something like this were to change, are you set up for success as best as possible?

We can't control the future. We know that, interestingly, the Senate did not push back on this as much as some people may have expected. So we have the House Bill that went to the Senate. And had this not been removed entirely, there was not going to be significant changes to what had been proposed, at least on early days.

So I say that we want to keep our eyes open and be mindful that things can change anytime. There's other sections of the bill that could potentially impact investors in other ways as well. So, keep our eyes open.

Greg Bonnell: I often think, in even our regular lives, when we dodge a bullet, we escape the worst-case scenario, it does make us rethink certain things about how we've been going. Is this an opportunity, maybe, to talk some about tax-efficient estate planning?

Nicole Ewing: Well, so from an estate planning perspective, interestingly, what the bill also contains-- the Big Beautiful Bill-- also contains is an increase to the US tax exemption amount for estate tax. And so Canadians do, potentially, have exposure to this. They also have a filing requirement.

So if you have US situs assets, which includes US securities, and you have more than US$60,000 value there, you're going to have to file a tax return or a report with the US government-- or your estate will need to do that, to the extent you are in excess of the exemption amount-- currently, $13.99 million-- set to sunset on December 31 down to half of that amount. If you have assets in excess of that, you would be subject to US estate tax.

So what the bill does now is increases the $13.99 million-- that was going to drop by half-- to $15 million going forward. And it will be indexed into the future. So this, essentially, is going to potentially reduce tax exposure. But also, it's a good reminder for Canadians that you are subject to these foreign rules. So we don't have Section 899. We don't have the changes to the dividend income and some of the other changes. But we do still have to be mindful that there is estate planning considerations if you have US exposure.

Greg Bonnell: You always do such a great job of breaking it down in terms that we can all understand. At the same time, these issues are complex. It sounds like if someone is listening to this conversation and saying, "OK, it's not going forward, there's still things I need to think about," maybe they should be talking to someone?

Nicole Ewing: I really suggest that. These are complex rules. And a lot of people were caught off-guard with this, just even knowing that there is withholding tax when you have US exposure in your non-reg accounts and other non-retirement accounts. And so, if you weren't aware of that-- because withholding sometimes happens-- it's behind the scenes, before the distributions are made to you.

And so if this is all new to you, get familiar with the current rules. Or, alternatively, you can outsource all of this concern, all of this worry, the requirement to stay up to date, and diligently be involved. So, hiring professionals to take that worry on for you and to make sure you have the expertise and the insights behind the scenes to make sure that they are looking out for your best interests is a great idea.

Original Post

data.title: Expeditors: An Under-The-Radar Compounder
data.description:
I have a bullish view of Expeditors (NYSE:EXPD), anchored on their phenomenal returns on equity and strengthened recently by the persistent undervaluation of the stock relative to the market. While the S&P 500 trades at a PE multiple of around 24, EXPD’s is under 20; such a discount is not really warranted, as I will make the case herein.

After initially presenting the long-term long thesis in January (here), the stock has gone nowhere. As a long-time holder, I view periods like this as a blessing, as the constant repurchases while the PE is well below historical averages have proven to be highly accretive.

Summary on thesis:

I must begin with the above-mentioned ROE. Through the years prior to the pandemic, ROE averaged 20%. This period excludes the distortion from the bumper years through the pandemic, which drives the long-term average up to 23%.

Any company can boost ROE by using leverage, a tactic that is sensible to a point but adds risk. Expeditors, however, has achieved their ROE without any debt – in fact, by continuously holding a large cash position ($1.3B as of March 31, 2025), which represents “negative debt”, their book equity is inflated somewhat, causing a drag on ROE.

Helping to keep capital low is the fact that Expeditors operates a very asset-light model, effectively building a global network of shipping clients to match with asset owners. The “buy cargo space in bulk and sell piecemeal” does not require high up-front costs; it does require a wide network with as many nodes and lanes as possible.

This is done by gradually opening regional offices and slowly taking share in a very fragmented (global) industry. Roll-up strategies don't work well when so much integration and so many jurisdictions are involved, but prudent and constant growth has proven a recipe for success.

Key to earning shipping business is the company’s global customs brokerage and services expertise. This allows for consolidation of services into one provider, simplifying logistics for customers and improving transparency and feedback during cargo transit. On the ship/airplane owner side, Expeditors is a key partner that can take large swaths of space all at once.

High returns and low risk are a powerful combination and point to superior skill in capital allocation. As argued in a prior piece, I believe a key ingredient is their unusual (and hard to replicate) compensation scheme. It aligns the whole organization around a single metric, operating income, which directly impacts bonuses (a material portion of yearly comp). This in turn allows for wide delegation to regional/district office managers, which cultivate client relationships from an empowered position.

The current relatively depressed multiple is the final piece of the puzzle. Although we never can be sure, this is likely the result of a combination of pandemic era bumper years normalizing on top of the more recent headlines around trade deals and potential disruptions. I prefer to focus on the numbers and let them inform us about what is going on at a company.

Recent results:

2024 was an important year in that growth returned to both revenues and shipping volumes. The situation ends the steady decline that followed the pandemic boost and sets a new base for future growth. 2024 revenues of $10.6B were 33% higher than revenues from 2019. Earnings for 2024 were $5.75/share. This is above 2019's $3.45, but below peak earnings of $8.37 during 2021.

Q1 results reflect a marked bounce back from depressed year-ago numbers. EPS was up 26%, to $1.47, on revenue of $2.1B, up 21%. Importantly, both ocean and airfreight volumes were up nearly 10% each. Repurchases were a little bit lower than expected ($177 million) but understandable given volatility in operating environment (it was not due to lower cash flow, cash balances increased to $1.3B from $1.1B in December).

Management updates:

CEO Jeffrey Musser retired this year, handing the reins to long-time company insider Daniel R. Wall. Since it is not their practice to do earnings calls, I usually look for the 8-K on selected questions, the latest was posted in mid-May; the first under Wall's tenure.

The tone is quite positive, the volatility around tariffs is driving more customer engagement, a providing opportunity for share gains. The rest of 2025 will likely continue to be full of surprises, but that is precisely when the company tends to do best. One thing to look for during the next (Q2) release is for commentary around buy/sell rates, which have been compressing, as well as volumes, but these will continue to be impacted on a short-term basis due to tariff negotiation windows and related pull forwards.

Valuation:

As noted, PE continues to be depressed when compared to the market and when compared to past levels. This despite the low risk (no debt) and much higher than average ROE.

I believe management will continue to use share repurchases liberally to take advantage. The charts below show how impactful the past repurchases have been, increasing earnings per share from around $3 in the 2015-2020 period to $6 currently (in a weaker part of the cycle).

Share repurchases will continue to be a tailwind for EPS growth and also help keep dividend growth higher. Although the yield always looks low, the growth has been impressive - for long-term holders, it is the growth that matters more than current yields.

Keep in mind, the dividend is paid semi-annually; thus, the $0.77 level equates to $1.54.

Risks

A key question I have been asking myself as I review my own portfolio is: how will AI impact operations? For Expeditor's I believe the initial implementation is going to be decidedly positive.

Salaries are the largest expense (other than freight), fluctuation from $2B to $1.7B more recently. Although warehouse operations are not necessarily as core to Expeditors as fulfillment behemoths like Amazon (AMZN); It is good to see they have been using automation for a while (aided by AI for picking route efficiency).

It is not hard to imagine how most processes can be enhanced by AI, including routing efficiencies, pricing algorithms and package handling optimization. But at the core, the business is consolidating customers seeking solutions and purchasing cargo ahead of time and in bulk.

Other than a recession, where trade volumes inevitably slow down, the balance sheet is a fortress and management has flexibility on headcount and low fixed costs. The company is built to last and usually comes out stronger (in part by an improved negotiating position vis-à-vis asset owners who are hurt more directly).

Conclusion:

Expeditors appears undervalued, as its below-market PE persists despite their leading ROE coupled with no leverage. The situation should not distress long-term holders as the company will very likely continue to repurchase shares, already over 35% of shares have been retired - a strong tailwind to EPS and dividend growth. In fact, during Jeff Musser's tenure as CEO, Expeditors returned almost $10B in repurchases and dividends, about 2/3 of its current market cap (in just 10 years!).

Going forward, I expect EPS through the cycle to comfortably surpass $5/share and perhaps settle around $6/share. The stock, trading at $120, needs to be adjusted for $10/share in cash, resulting in a sub-20 PE for a +20% ROE.

data.title: RTX Stock Flies Past Targets: Why I'm Raising My Price Target
data.description:
RTX Corporation (RTX), a leading aerospace and defense company, has strongly outperformed the market since my 2024 Buy rating. The stock has surged 18.4%, easily beating the S&P 500’s 2.7% gain, and delivered an even stronger 19.9% return compared to the post-tariff stock price dip discussed in my prior report. These gains pushed RTX above both my conservative and more optimistic price targets. In this report, I analyze RTX’s recent performance and fundamentals to determine whether the stock still offers attractive upside and deserves a continued Buy rating.

The Bullish Case For Commercial Aerospace And Defense

The bullish case for the commercial aerospace and defense industries is quite an easy one. Demand for commercial airplanes remains strong, with Boeing (BA) and Airbus (OTCPK:EADSF) (OTCPK:EADSY) having thousands of airplanes in backlog. Boeing has a backlog of over 6,500 airplanes, while competitor Airbus has a backlog of over 8,630 airplanes. Boeing projects demand for new airplanes to be 43,600 airplanes over the next 20 years. This provides an extremely strong backdrop for OEMs and the supply chain.

For defense, an unpredictable geopolitical landscape with various conflict zones globally pushes defense spendings higher. Europe is working on increasing its security and defense autonomy, which does offer opportunities for US defense contractors in parallel with European contractors. At 3.5% of GDP, the defense budget could grow to $761 billion by 2030 and obviously with the new NATO targets of 5% that could rise to over $1 trillion. In the US, the national security budget is already set to exceed $1 trillion.

The Side Notes To The Bullish Case In Commercial Aerospace

The long-term trends in both end markets are very positive. However, there are side notes both positive and negative that should be kept in mind. Probably the biggest challenge that RTX is facing is with its GTF engine deliveries. Due to metal powder issues, the company has taken significant charges in 2023, but the operational impact lasts through 2025 and some airline executives are anticipating impacts beyond that. Currently, RTX is compensating airlines, which provides a financial drag and deliveries of Airbus likely driven by the GTF engine issues are also lower year-on-year. Furthermore, we note that second quarter cash flow will be affected by the strike at Pratt & Whitney. A second concern would be the tariffs. Initially, RTX expected a $850 million headwind but with tariff pauses, mitigations and USMCA coverage things are looking a bit better.

Looking at Boeing, RTX does not provide the engines for the Boeing 737 MAX, but it has significant exposure for all Boeing’s commercial airplanes through avionics and cabins. Boeing hoped to see the production rate cap of 38 per month for the MAX lifted by mid-2025, but in the aftermath of the unrelated Air India Boeing 787 crash, chances for that cap to be lifted now have slimmed.

In the aerospace supply chain, things are improving for the Dreamliner program where heat exchangers were a bottleneck, but we do note that cabins and predominantly seats have been a pain point and I believe that as OEMs look to increase production that will remain a pace item.

Regarding the acquisition of RTX’s actuation business by Safran (OTCPK:SAFRF)(OTCPK:SAFRY), the Department of Justice has required Safran to divest assets. This should pave the way for the divestiture to be completed after approval in Europe and Brazil has also been obtained. The company has also reached an agreement with TransDigm (TDG) to sell Simmonds Precisions Products for $765 million.

So, the long-term bullish thesis stands, but there are a lot of moving items involved.

Defense Budget Allocation Provides A Risk

The defense end market is strong. However, there are also various risks involved. One major risk at this point is the allocation of budgets. We see big projects being awarded such as NGAD (Next Generation Air Dominance) and the Golden Dome. These programs provide opportunities for RTX through its propulsion, radar and missile defense programs with high demand for missiles as well. At the same time, we do note that somewhat against my expectations the Pentagon has reduced its requested number of F-35s for which RTX provides the engines. Congress can still intervene and appropriate more funds for the F-35 program, but in case that does not happen, there is some risk for RTX.

Investment Case For RTX Remains Compelling For Long-Term Investors

So, while the long-term trends look favorable, we also do note that besides opportunities, there also are some near-term risks for RTX that we should carefully consider when valuing the company.

To determine multi-year price targets, The Aerospace Forum has developed a stock screener which uses a combination of analyst consensus on EBITDA, cash flows and the most recent balance sheet data. Each quarter, we revisit those assumptions and update the stock price targets accordingly. In a separate blog, I have detailed our analysis methodology.

The investment case for RTX is extremely compelling if we consider the company’s median EV/EBITDA. In fact, there is no upside left this year. The market is forward-looking, and so we should also consider what future years provide in terms of valuation. The upside in the years ahead is not huge, with 4% for 2026 and 14% towards FY2027 earnings. Normally, this would result in a hold rating at best. However, we should also consider that RTX trades at a discount compared to peers. I do not expect that gap to close fully, but we do note that the company has significantly improved productivity and is infusing a digital backbone that allows the organization to become more efficient. If we let the trade between the peer group and the company median for FY2025, we still have 19% upside and a $171.50 price target. The increase in the price target is driven by a 4% to 6% improvement in EBITDA, as tariff impacts are somewhat less impactful than previously expected. Furthermore, free cash flow expectations also improved in line with that, and we have the expected proceeds from the divestiture of Simmond Precision Products. We also note that the trading multiple for aerospace and defense companies has improved.

Conclusion: RTX Remains Compelling On Multiple Expansion And Long-Term Growth

While there are some near term pressures, I believe that RTX remains somewhat undervalued. The biggest item that the company has to deal with are the GTF engine issues, and that remains a watch item. However, at the same time, we do note that the long-term trends remain favorable and RTX is working on improving efficiency. All of that is happening while the company is trading at a steep discount to peers and I do not see a strong justification for that discount for the simple reason that in terms of performance and importance RTX is a major player that is not showing subpar operational performance and the engine issues do not justify a steep discount against peers. As a result, I am raising my price target from $149 to $171.50.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.
